title: Point/Vector: unit tests
analysis:
Task 227 requires >=22 tests covering Point/Vector construction, all arithmetic rules from spec section 3.3.1, component access, dimensional checking, scalar-vector multiplication, P+P error, different-N error, undef propagation, and the specific Point3<Length>-Point3<Length>->Vector3<Length> case.

The existing point_vector_eval_tests.rs has 12 component-access tests from task 226. Task 225 ('affine arithmetic') is marked done but the eval_add/eval_sub/eval_mul/eval_div/eval_unop functions in lib.rs do NOT currently handle Value::Tensor — they fall through to `_ => Value::Undef`. This means the Tensor arithmetic implementation is missing and must be added alongside the tests (true TDD: write failing test, then implement).

At runtime, both Point and Vector are represented as Value::Tensor(Vec<Value>). The type distinction exists only in the Type enum (Type::Point vs Type::Vector). The evaluator's arithmetic functions (eval_add, eval_sub, etc.) operate on Values without type info, so component-wise Tensor operations are type-agnostic. The Point+Point error guard requires checking CompiledExpr.result_type in eval_binop before dispatching.

The implementation adds: (1) Tensor component-wise add/sub with length checking, (2) Scalar*Tensor and Tensor*Scalar in eval_mul, (3) Tensor/Scalar in eval_div, (4) Tensor negation in eval_unop, (5) Point+Point type guard in eval_binop. All follow strict Undef propagation: if any component op yields Undef, the entire Tensor result is Undef.

steps.description: Write 4 construction tests verifying Value::Tensor creation for Point3<Length>, Vector3<Length>, Point2<Length>, Vector2<Dimensionless>. These pass immediately since Value::Tensor already exists. Tests verify component count and values match. Add to point_vector_eval_tests.rs in a new '// --- Construction ---' section.

Write 7 failing tests for Tensor add/sub arithmetic: (1) vector3_add_vector3_componentwise, (2) vector3_sub_vector3_componentwise, (3) point3_add_vector3_returns_point, (4) vector3_add_point3_returns_point, (5) point3_sub_point3_returns_vector, (6) point3_length_sub_point3_length_gives_vector3_length (the specific spec case), (7) vector3_add_vector2_n_mismatch_returns_undef. All use CompiledExpr::binop with BinOp::Add/Sub on Tensor literals. Tests 1-6 fail because eval_add/eval_sub don't handle Tensor. Test 7 passes by accident (Undef fallthrough).

Add Tensor component-wise add/sub to eval_add and eval_sub in lib.rs. Pattern: match (Value::Tensor(a), Value::Tensor(b)) => check a.len()==b.len() (else Undef), zip and apply eval_add/eval_sub per component, if any component is Undef return Undef, else return Value::Tensor(results). This makes step-2 tests 1-6 pass. Test 7 (N mismatch) passes via the length check.

Write 1 failing test: point3_add_point3_returns_undef — create two Point3<Length> Tensor literals, add via BinOp::Add with result_type Type::point3(Type::length()). After step-3, Tensor+Tensor works, so P+P now returns a Tensor instead of Undef. This test fails, proving the guard is needed.

Add Point+Point type guard in eval_binop in lib.rs. Before dispatching BinOp::Add to eval_add, check if both left.result_type and right.result_type match Type::Point{..}. If so, return Value::Undef immediately. This makes step-4 test pass while preserving all other Tensor add behavior.

Write 3 failing tests for scalar-vector multiplication and division: (1) scalar_mul_vector3_scales_components — Scalar(2.0,LENGTH) * Vector3<Length>(1,2,3) -> Tensor([2,4,6] with AREA dimension), (2) vector3_mul_scalar_commutative — same but reversed operand order, (3) vector3_div_scalar_divides_components — Vector3<Length>(10,20,30) / Scalar(2.0,DIMENSIONLESS) -> Tensor([5,10,15]). All fail because eval_mul/eval_div don't handle Tensor.

Add Scalar*Tensor, Tensor*Scalar to eval_mul and Tensor/Scalar to eval_div in lib.rs. For mul: match (Value::Tensor(components), scalar) or (scalar, Value::Tensor(components)) where scalar is Scalar/Int/Real, map each component through eval_mul(component, scalar). For div: match (Value::Tensor(components), scalar), map each component through eval_div(component, scalar). Apply strict Undef propagation. Makes step-6 tests pass.

Write 1 failing test: negate_vector3_negates_all_components — CompiledExpr::unop(UnOp::Neg, Vector3<Length>(1,2,3), Type::vec3(Type::length())). Expected: Tensor([-1,-2,-3]) with LENGTH dimension. Fails because eval_unop Neg doesn't handle Tensor.

Add Tensor negation to eval_unop in lib.rs. In the UnOp::Neg match arm, add: Value::Tensor(components) => map each component through negation (call eval_unop-style negation per component or inline match), strict Undef propagation. Makes step-8 test pass.

Write 4 tests for dimension checking and Undef propagation: (1) vector3_add_different_dimension_returns_undef — Vector3<Length> + Vector3<Angle> -> Undef (component-wise Scalar add checks dimension, strict propagation makes whole Tensor Undef), (2) scalar_times_vector_combines_dimensions — Scalar(2.0,MASS) * Vector3<Length>(1,2,3) -> Tensor with MASS*LENGTH dimension on each component, (3) undef_operand_in_tensor_binop_propagates — Undef + Vector3 -> Undef (handled by existing strict propagation in eval_binop), (4) tensor_with_undef_component_in_add_propagates — Vector3 with one Undef component + Vector3 -> Undef (strict propagation in Tensor add). All should pass with existing implementation from prior steps.

design_decisions.decision: Tensor arithmetic is value-level, not type-level
design_decisions.rationale: eval_add/sub/mul/div operate on Values without type information. At runtime, both Point and Vector are Value::Tensor(Vec<Value>). Component-wise operations are type-agnostic. This keeps the evaluator simple and aligns with the existing scalar arithmetic pattern.

design_decisions.decision: Point+Point guard uses CompiledExpr.result_type in eval_binop
design_decisions.rationale: The only place where Point vs Vector distinction matters at eval time is the P+P prohibition. Since eval_binop receives full CompiledExpr (which has result_type: Type), we check types there before dispatching to eval_add. This is a surgical check that doesn't complicate the arithmetic functions.

design_decisions.decision: Strict Undef propagation for Tensor operations
design_decisions.rationale: If any component operation returns Undef, the entire Tensor result is Undef. This follows the existing strict propagation pattern (eval_binop returns Undef if either operand is Undef, eval_sum returns Undef if any item is Undef). Dimension mismatch within components naturally produces Undef through the existing Scalar dimension check.

design_decisions.decision: Scalar*Tensor detects scalar-like values by matching Value variants
design_decisions.rationale: Rather than introducing a helper method, eval_mul matches (Value::Tensor, Value::Scalar/Int/Real) and the reverse. This is consistent with how eval_mul already handles Scalar*Int and Scalar*Real cases. The per-component mul reuses the existing eval_mul function recursively.

design_decisions.decision: N-mismatch check uses Vec length comparison
design_decisions.rationale: Different-N Tensors have different Vec lengths at runtime. Checking a.len() != b.len() in the Tensor add/sub match arm is simple, correct, and produces Undef consistently with other error cases.

design_decisions.decision: Tests extend existing point_vector_eval_tests.rs rather than creating a new file
design_decisions.rationale: The existing file has 12 component-access tests and established patterns (imports, helpers). Adding arithmetic tests in clearly labeled sections keeps related tests together and avoids import duplication.

reuse.how: Use these helpers to construct dimensioned Scalar values for Tensor components in test literals, avoiding manual DimensionVector construction.
Use these shorthands for result_type annotations in CompiledExpr::binop and literal construction.
Use the existing expression builders to construct test expressions. binop takes (BinOp, left, right, result_type) and unop takes (UnOp, operand, result_type).
All tests use this pattern for evaluation context with no user-defined functions, matching the existing 12 component-access tests.

Component-wise Tensor add/sub reuses eval_add/eval_sub per component. Dimension mismatches between Vector<Length> and Vector<Angle> are caught by the existing Scalar(ad != bd) check, returning Undef per component, which triggers strict Tensor-level Undef propagation.
The existing 'if lv.is_undef() || rv.is_undef() { return Value::Undef }' guard handles Undef operand propagation for Tensor binops without any new code.
Scalar*Tensor dimension combination is handled automatically: eval_mul on (Scalar{dim_a}, Scalar{dim_b}) uses dim_a.mul(dim_b) to combine exponents. No new dimension logic needed.
